unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 7: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth. In this unit you will explore what it means to succeed, how people build meaningful careers, and how personal growth shapes the choices we make. You will read about real-world career paths, discuss what makes a person truly successful, and discover that success is not only about money or fame — it is also about dedication, resilience, and the contribution you make to your community.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the five mini-lessons in the Grammar file (Adjective + Preposition, Verb Patterns, Passive Voice, and more).
Read the Story file, “The Storm of Truth,” and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.

visual.alt: Learners discussing success, careers and personal growth

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A strong desire to achieve something important in life.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Her ambition is to become a doctor who helps children in rural areas.
practiceSentences: Her ambition is to become a doctor who helps children in rural areas.
The word ambition belongs to our success and achievement vocabulary.
I can explain ambition in my own words.
I can connect ambition to this unit's texts.
I can use ambition in a clear Grade 7 sentence.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Read and understand a passage about careers and success, and discuss its ideas in English.
Use adjective + preposition combinations correctly (e.g., *interested in*, *proud of*, *responsible for*).
Understand and use verb patterns with infinitives (*to do*) and gerunds (*doing*).
Form and use the passive voice to describe achievements and processes.
Write a biography of someone you admire or a career presentation about your dream job.
Speak clearly when presenting ideas about success, goals, and career paths.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 7: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth. In this unit you will explore what it means to succeed, how people build meaningful careers, and how personal growth shapes the choices we make. You will read about real-world career paths, discuss what makes a person truly successful, and discover that success is not only about money or fame — it is also about dedication, resilience, and the contribution you make to your community.
This unit brings together reading, grammar, vocabulary, writing, and speaking into one rich topic. You will study important grammar patterns: adjective + preposition combinations (such as *proud of*, *responsible for*, *dedicated to*), verb patterns with infinitives and gerunds, and the passive voice. These tools will help you write and speak about achievements, careers, and personal experiences in polished, confident English.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a passage about careers and success, and discuss its ideas in English.
Use adjective + preposition combinations correctly (e.g., *interested in*, *proud of*, *responsible for*).
Understand and use verb patterns with infinitives (*to do*) and gerunds (*doing*).
Form and use the passive voice to describe achievements and processes.
Write a biography of someone you admire or a career presentation about your dream job.
Speak clearly when presenting ideas about success, goals, and career paths.
Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently.
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the five mini-lessons in the Grammar file (Adjective + Preposition, Verb Patterns, Passive Voice, and more).
Read the Story file, “The Storm of Truth,” and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it.
ambition — a strong desire to achieve something important in life.
dedication — being fully committed to a task or purpose.
perseverance — continuing to work hard even when things are difficult.
achievement — something that has been accomplished through effort.
milestone — an important event or stage in the development of something.
distinction — excellence that makes someone stand out; a high mark or honour.
profession — a job that requires special training or education.
vocation — a strong feeling of being suited to a particular career or calling.
apprentice — a person learning a trade or skill from an experienced worker.
entrepreneur — a person who starts and runs their own business.
qualification — a skill, quality, or certificate that makes someone suitable for a job.
mentorship — guidance and support given by an experienced person to someone less experienced.
resilience — the ability to recover from difficulties and keep going.
integrity — being honest and having strong moral principles.
contribution — something you give or do to help achieve something or to help others.
aspiration — a hope or dream of achieving something in the future.
gratitude — the feeling of being thankful and appreciating what you have.

readings.title: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
initiative — the ability to see what needs to be done and do it without being told.
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the bold vocabulary words. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions.
In communities across East Africa, young people are discovering that success does not always follow a single path. Some become doctors or engineers after years of study. Others find their vocation through hands-on work, learning a trade as an apprentice beside an experienced craftsperson. What matters most is not the title you hold but the dedication you bring to your work and the contribution you make to the people around you.
Nadia, a seventeen-year-old from a small town near Garowe, always dreamed of becoming a teacher. Her family did not have much money, but Nadia was determined to succeed. She studied by lamplight every evening, and her perseverance impressed everyone who knew her. When she passed her exams with distinction, the entire neighbourhood celebrated. “I am proud of what I have achieved,” Nadia told a local reporter, “but I am even more grateful for the people who helped me along the way.”
Nadia’s story shows that ambition alone is not enough. She was supported by her mother, who sold vegetables at the market to pay for books. She was encouraged by a teacher who stayed after school to give extra lessons. And she was inspired by a visiting speaker who said, “Every great career begins with a single step of courage.” That speaker was herself once a young girl from a rural village who went on to become a university professor.
Not far from Nadia’s town, a young man named Noah chose a different route. He was always fascinated by machines and electronics. Instead of attending university, he became an apprentice at a small repair shop. His mentor taught him to fix radios, solar panels, and mobile phones. Within two years, Noah had saved enough money to open his own workshop. Today he is a successful entrepreneur who employs three other young people.
“People told me that working with my hands was not a real profession,” Noah said. “But I am passionate about what I do, and my business helps the community. Solar panels are repaired here. Phones are fixed here. Jobs are created here.” He paused and added, “Success is not measured by a certificate on the wall. It is measured by the lives you improve.”
Both Nadia and Noah show that success requires resilience. Nadia faced nights without electricity and months without new textbooks. Noah was criticised by people who thought his work was unimportant. But both kept going because they were committed to their goals and responsible for their own futures.
A career counsellor at a secondary school in Hargeisa explains: “We teach our students that there are many kinds of success. A doctor saves lives. A farmer feeds families. A builder creates homes. An artist inspires hearts. Each profession deserves respect, and each person’s aspiration is valid. What we encourage is that students find something they are interested in, work hard at it, and never stop learning.”
The counsellor also stresses the importance of mentorship. “Young people need guides,” she says. “A good mentor does not tell you what to do — they help you discover what you are capable of. When someone believes in you, you start to believe in yourself.”

readings.title: The Storm of Truth, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The sky above Lamu was heavy with grey clouds when Sarah arrived at school that Monday morning. The wind carried the sharp smell of salt from the Indian Ocean, and the palm trees along the road swayed as if they were whispering a warning.
"Something big is coming," said her classmate, Noah, looking up at the darkening sky. "The fishermen pulled their boats in early."
But Sarah was not thinking about the weather. Her mind was fixed on something else entirely: the Regional Young Leaders Competition. The winner would receive a scholarship to attend a special leadership academy in Nairobi — a milestone that could change her life forever.
"I have been preparing for this for months," Sarah told Noah. "My speech is about how young people can build careers that serve their communities."
"You are dedicated to this," Noah said, nodding. "I have never seen anyone work so hard."
Sarah smiled, but inside she felt the weight of expectation. Her family was proud of her ambition, and her mother had told her, "We are grateful for every opportunity you earn. Make the most of it."
At school, their English teacher, Mr. Adam, announced some news.
"The competition semi-final is this Friday," he said. "Three students from our school have been selected: Sarah, Noah, and Nadia. You will each give a five-minute speech in front of a panel of judges."
Sarah glanced at Nadia. They had been friends since childhood, but recently something had changed. Nadia had become distant and secretive. She carried a folder everywhere and would not show anyone what was inside.
"I am committed to winning," Nadia said quietly. "This qualification could open doors for me."
That evening, Sarah worked on her speech at the community centre. The centre had been established by a retired teacher named Noah Adam, who now served as a mentor to the town’s young people. His small office was filled with books, maps, and photographs of students who had gone on to become doctors, engineers, and teachers.
"Mentorship is about more than advice," Noah Adam had once told them. "It is about helping someone discover what they are capable of."
Sarah read her speech aloud to him. It was about how entrepreneurship could lift communities out of poverty, and how young apprentices could learn skills that schools did not always teach.
"Good structure," Noah Adam said. "But I want you to add something personal. Talk about your own aspiration. What drives you? Why does this matter to you, Sarah?"
She nodded and wrote a new paragraph that night. In it she described watching her mother sell fruit at the harbour, working from dawn until dusk, and how that resilience had taught her that success was not given — it was built.
On Tuesday, the storm warnings grew louder. The administration announced that all after-school activities would be cancelled if the weather worsened. Rain began to fall in bursts, and the harbour was closed.
But inside the school, a different kind of storm was brewing.
During lunch, Sarah went to the library to return a book. As she turned the corner between the shelves, she heard voices. Nadia was speaking to someone on the phone.
"Yes, I have it," Nadia said. "The speech is very good. I just changed a few words so it does not look exactly the same. No one will know."

readings.title: The Storm of Truth, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Sarah froze. Her heart hammered in her chest.
Nadia looked up and saw her. For a moment, neither of them spoke.
"Sarah — it is not what you think," Nadia started.
"Whose speech did you copy?" Sarah asked, her voice steady but low.
Nadia’s eyes dropped. "It was found online. A speech by a student in Tanzania who won a competition two years ago. I changed some sentences. I — I did not have time to write my own."
"You could have asked for help," Sarah said. "Mr. Adam would have helped you. Noah Adam would have helped you."
"I was afraid of looking weak," Nadia whispered. "Everyone expects me to be brilliant. My family keeps telling me I must win. I was worried about disappointing them."
Sarah felt a mix of anger and sadness. She understood the pressure. She felt it too. But she also knew that integrity mattered more than any prize.
"If you give that speech," Sarah said gently, "and someone discovers the truth later, everything you have worked for will be lost. Your reputation will be damaged. Is that worth it?"
Nadia was silent for a long time. Then she said, "What should I do?"
"Write your own speech," Sarah said. "You still have three days. I will help you."
That evening, the storm arrived in full force. Thunder cracked across the sky, and rain battered the rooftops of Lamu. The power went out across the entire town. Families gathered in their homes with candles and oil lamps, waiting for the weather to pass.
But in Sarah’s house, something remarkable was happening. By the glow of a kerosene lamp, Sarah and Nadia sat together at the kitchen table, writing.
"Tell me what you are passionate about," Sarah said.
"The sea," Nadia replied. "I love the ocean. I want to study marine science. I want to protect the coral reefs and the fish that our community depends on."
"Then that is your speech," Sarah said.
Over the next two days, Nadia wrote and rewrote her speech. She practised it in front of Noah Adam, who gave her honest feedback. She was encouraged by his words: "This is real. This is you. A speech is valued for its truth, not its polish."
On Thursday evening, the storm finally passed. The sky cleared, and the stars appeared over the ocean. The town began to clean up: fallen branches were removed by neighbours, damaged market stalls were repaired by shopkeepers, and the school hall was prepared for the competition the next day.
Friday morning arrived bright and warm. The judges — a university professor, a local business leader, and a journalist — sat at a long table in the school hall. Students from five schools had gathered.
Noah spoke first. His speech was about the importance of vocational training and how apprenticeships could help young people who could not afford university. He was confident in his message and spoke with clear distinction.
Then Nadia took the stage. Her hands were shaking, but her voice was strong.
"My name is Nadia, and I am fascinated by the ocean," she began. "Every day, I watch the fishermen go out, and I wonder: what is happening beneath the surface? Our reefs are dying. Our fish are disappearing. And not enough young people are being trained in marine science to help."

readings.title: The Storm of Truth, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
She spoke about her dream to study coral ecosystems, about the contribution she wanted to make, and about the moment she almost lost her way.
"I nearly gave a speech that was not mine," she said quietly. The hall went still. "But a true friend reminded me that integrity is the foundation of every real achievement. I am standing here today because I chose to be honest. And that, to me, is already a kind of success."
The applause was loud and long.
Finally, Sarah stepped up. She spoke about her mother, about entrepreneurship, about how every profession deserves respect. She spoke about perseverance — how she had written her speech seven times before she was satisfied with it.
"Success is not a single moment," she said. "It is not a trophy or a certificate. It is the dedication you show every day. It is the gratitude you feel for those who help you. And it is the initiative you take when no one is watching."
When the judges announced the results, Sarah was named the winner. But as she stepped forward to accept the certificate, she looked back at Nadia and smiled.
Later, Noah Adam found them both under the old mango tree in the schoolyard.
"You were both brave today," he said. "Nadia, you were brave because you told the truth. Sarah, you were brave because you helped your friend instead of protecting your advantage."
"Is that not what mentorship is?" Sarah asked.
Noah Adam laughed. "I think the student is now teaching the teacher."
That night, the ocean was calm. The storm had passed, and the truth had held.
Sarah wrote one last line in her notebook before sleep:
"The storm tested us. But the truth held us steady. Every achievement built on integrity is a milestone that no wind can blow away."

grammar.title: Adjective + Preposition — The Basics
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
In English, many adjectives must be followed by a specific preposition. You cannot choose just any preposition — each adjective has a fixed partner. For example, we say interested in (not “interested about”) and proud of (not “proud for”). Learning these pairs will make your English sound natural and help you write accurately about success, careers, and personal growth.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
In English, many adjectives must be followed by a specific preposition. You cannot choose just any preposition — each adjective has a fixed partner. For example, we say interested in (not “interested about”) and proud of (not “proud for”). Learning these pairs will make your English sound natural and help you write accurately about success, careers, and personal growth.
Key adjective + preposition pairs
Adjective + OF:
proud of — She is proud of her results.
capable of — You are capable of achieving great things.
aware of — He is aware of the difficulties ahead.
afraid of — Do not be afraid of making mistakes.
fond of — I am fond of reading about famous scientists.
ashamed of — There is nothing to be ashamed of in asking for help.
Adjective + IN:
interested in — She is interested in studying law.
involved in — He is involved in the school’s career fair.
skilled in — She is skilled in public speaking.
successful in — He was successful in starting his business.
confident in — I am confident in my abilities.
Adjective + FOR:
responsible for — You are responsible for your own progress.
famous for — The city is famous for its universities.
grateful for — I am grateful for my teacher’s guidance.
prepared for — Are you prepared for the exam?
qualified for — She is qualified for the position.
Adjective + TO:
dedicated to — He is dedicated to his studies.
committed to — She is committed to helping others.
related to — This lesson is related to career planning.
accustomed to — She is accustomed to working long hours.
Adjective + BY / ABOUT / WITH:
amazed by — We were amazed by her achievement.
inspired by — He was inspired by his grandfather’s story.
fascinated by — She is fascinated by marine science.
worried about — He is worried about the future.
passionate about — She is passionate about teaching.
satisfied with — I am satisfied with my progress.
disappointed with — He was disappointed with his results.
A common mistake
Mistake: “I am interested about science.” ✘
Fix: “I am interested in science.” ✔
Mistake: “She is proud for her daughter.” ✘
Fix: “She is proud of her daughter.” ✔
Memory tip
Learn adjective + preposition pairs as mini phrases. Write each pair on a small card (front: the adjective, back: the preposition + an example sentence). Practise them daily.
Practice 1
Complete each sentence with the correct preposition:
She is very proud ______ her brother’s success.
We are interested ______ learning about different careers.
He is responsible ______ organising the school event.
I am grateful ______ the support I received.
They were amazed ______ the speaker’s story.
She is dedicated ______ becoming a doctor.
He was disappointed ______ his performance in the exam.
Are you prepared ______ the interview tomorrow?
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply adjective + preposition — the basics in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Adjective + Preposition — Advanced Uses
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
Now that you know the basic adjective + preposition pairs, let us look at some patterns that will help you use them even more confidently.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
Now that you know the basic adjective + preposition pairs, let us look at some patterns that will help you use them even more confidently.
Adjective + preposition + gerund (-ing)
When a verb follows an adjective + preposition, the verb must be in the -ing form (the gerund). This is because prepositions are always followed by a noun or gerund, never by an infinitive.
She is good at solving problems. (not “good at to solve”)
He is interested in becoming an engineer. (not “interested in to become”)
I am tired of waiting for results. (not “tired of to wait”)
She is responsible for looking after the library.
He is committed to improving his grades.
We are excited about starting the new project.
Adjective + preposition in questions
In questions, the preposition usually stays with the adjective:
What are you interested in?
Who is responsible for this project?
What are you afraid of?
What is she passionate about?
A common mistake
Mistake: “I am interested in to study medicine.” ✘
Fix: “I am interested in studying medicine.” ✔
Remember: preposition + verb = always use the -ing form.
Practice 2
Complete each sentence using the correct form of the verb in brackets:
She is good at ______ (solve) difficult maths problems.
He is interested in ______ (become) a pilot.
I am tired of ______ (hear) excuses.
They are excited about ______ (visit) the new science lab.
She is responsible for ______ (manage) the team.
Are you used to ______ (wake) up early for school?
He is committed to ______ (learn) a new skill every month.
What are you passionate about ______ (do) in the future?
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply adjective + preposition — advanced uses in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Verb Patterns — Infinitive and Gerund
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
When one verb follows another in English, the second verb takes a specific form. Some verbs are followed by the infinitive (to + verb), some by the gerund (verb + -ing), and some can take both. Knowing which pattern to use is essential when writing about plans, ambitions, and experiences.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
When one verb follows another in English, the second verb takes a specific form. Some verbs are followed by the infinitive (to + verb), some by the gerund (verb + -ing), and some can take both. Knowing which pattern to use is essential when writing about plans, ambitions, and experiences.
Verbs followed by the infinitive (to + verb)
want — I want to become a teacher.
hope — She hopes to win the scholarship.
decide — He decided to study harder.
plan — We plan to visit the university.
agree — They agreed to work together.
learn — She learned to code last year.
promise — He promised to help his classmates.
offer — The teacher offered to stay after school.
refuse — She refused to give up.
manage — He managed to pass the exam.
Verbs followed by the gerund (verb + -ing)
enjoy — She enjoys reading biographies.
practise — He practises speaking every day.
avoid — Try to avoid wasting time.
consider — Have you considered changing careers?
finish — I finished writing my application.
keep — Keep trying until you succeed.
suggest — She suggested starting a study group.
mind — Would you mind helping me?
miss — I miss living near the school.
deny — He denied copying the homework.
Verbs that take both (with a change in meaning)
remember to do = do not forget to do it: “Remember to bring your CV to the interview.”
remember doing = recall a past event: “I remember meeting the principal for the first time.”
stop to do = pause one thing in order to do another: “He stopped to help a younger student.”
stop doing = quit an activity: “She stopped worrying and took action.”
try to do = attempt something difficult: “I tried to solve the puzzle.”
try doing = experiment with something: “Try using a dictionary for new words.”
A common mistake
Mistake: “I enjoy to read books.” ✘
Fix: “I enjoy reading books.” ✔
Mistake: “She decided studying medicine.” ✘
Fix: “She decided to study medicine.” ✔
Memory tip
Make two lists in your notebook: one for TO verbs and one for -ING verbs. Add new verbs as you meet them. Review the lists every week.
Practice 3
Choose the correct form to complete each sentence:
She wants ______ (to become / becoming) a scientist.
He enjoys ______ (to teach / teaching) children.
They decided ______ (to move / moving) to the city.
I finished ______ (to write / writing) my essay.
He offered ______ (to carry / carrying) the heavy books.
She suggested ______ (to start / starting) a book club.
I remember ______ (to visit / visiting) my grandmother when I was young.
Please remember ______ (to lock / locking) the door when you leave.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply verb patterns — infinitive and gerund in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Passive Voice
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The passive voice is used when we want to focus on the action itself or on the person or thing that receives the action, rather than on who performed it. It is very common in formal writing, news reports, descriptions of processes, and academic English. In this unit, you will use it to describe achievements, career paths, and community events.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The passive voice is used when we want to focus on the action itself or on the person or thing that receives the action, rather than on who performed it. It is very common in formal writing, news reports, descriptions of processes, and academic English. In this unit, you will use it to describe achievements, career paths, and community events.
How to form it
The passive is formed with the verb be (in the correct tense) + the past participle of the main verb. The doer of the action (the agent) can be added with by, but it is often left out when it is unknown, unimportant, or obvious.
Present simple passive:
“The award is given to the top student every year.”
“Applications are reviewed by the committee.”
Past simple passive:
“The school was built in 1995.”
“She was awarded a scholarship.”
Present continuous passive:
“New classrooms are being built this month.”
“Students are being trained in new skills.”
Present perfect passive:
“The winner has been announced.”
“The road has been repaired.”
Changing active to passive
To change a sentence from active to passive:
Find the object of the active sentence — it becomes the subject.
Change the verb to be + past participle in the same tense.
Move the original subject to a by phrase (or leave it out).
Example:
Active: “The committee chose the winner.”
Passive: “The winner was chosen (by the committee).”
A common mistake
Mistake: “The prize was give to Samira.” ✘ (Wrong past participle.)
Fix: “The prize was given to Samira.” ✔
Mistake: “The students is taught by Mr. Adam.” ✘ (Wrong form of be.)
Fix: “The students are taught by Mr. Adam.” ✔
Memory tip
Think of the passive voice as putting a spotlight on the receiver of the action. In “The cake was eaten,” the cake is in the spotlight. In “She ate the cake,” she is in the spotlight. Choose which one matters more for your sentence.
Practice 4
Rewrite each sentence in the passive voice:
The judges selected three finalists.
The school offers free career workshops.
A local businessman has sponsored the competition.
They are building a new science laboratory.
The headteacher will announce the results tomorrow.
The community centre trained fifty young people last year.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply passive voice in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Putting It All Together
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
In real English writing and speaking, we combine many grammar features in the same text. This lesson helps you practise using adjective + preposition combinations, verb patterns, and the passive voice together — just as you will in the Writing Workshop.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
In real English writing and speaking, we combine many grammar features in the same text. This lesson helps you practise using adjective + preposition combinations, verb patterns, and the passive voice together — just as you will in the Writing Workshop.
Example paragraph
Read this paragraph and notice the grammar features:
“Samira is a young woman who is passionate about helping her community. She decided to train as a teacher because she enjoys working with children. After three years of hard work, she was awarded a teaching certificate. She is now responsible for a class of forty students. She is proud of what she has achieved, and she is committed to improving education in her town. New textbooks have been provided by a charity, and a computer room is being built this month.”
In this paragraph, find:
Four adjective + preposition combinations.
Two verb patterns (one infinitive, one gerund).
Two passive voice sentences.
Mixed Practice
Write your own paragraph (six to eight sentences) about a person — real or imagined — who has achieved something great. Your paragraph must include:
At least three different adjective + preposition combinations.
At least two verb patterns (one with infinitive, one with gerund).
At least two passive voice sentences.
At least three vocabulary words from Unit 7.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply putting it all together in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Grade 7 Editing and Application
grammar.explanation: Strong writers apply unit grammar while revising complete paragraphs for meaning, accuracy and flow.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read a paragraph aloud. Check sentence completeness, agreement, tense, joining words and punctuation. Revise one feature at a time.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: changing words without checking whether the paragraph still means the same thing. Fix: explain the purpose of every revision.
grammar.practice: Edit a four-paragraph response using the unit language focus. Mark eight changes and explain how four changes improve clarity and cohesion.

writing.title: Writing 1: Biography or Career Presentation
writing.promptAndInstructions:
In this section, you will choose one of two writing tasks. Both tasks let you practise vocabulary, grammar, and descriptive writing about success and careers.
Option A: Write a Biography
Write a short biography (150–200 words) about someone you admire. This could be a family member, a teacher, a community leader, or a famous person. Follow this structure:
Paragraph 1 — Introduction: Who is this person? Where are they from? Why do you admire them?
Paragraph 2 — Early life and challenges: What was their background? What difficulties did they face? Use adjective + preposition phrases (e.g., “She was determined to succeed despite the challenges.”).
Paragraph 3 — Achievements: What did they accomplish? Use the passive voice to describe their achievements (e.g., “She was awarded a scholarship.” “He was recognised for his work.”).
Paragraph 4 — Legacy and lessons: What can we learn from this person? What makes them a role model? End with a strong closing sentence.
Option B: Career Presentation
Write a career presentation (150–200 words) about your dream job or a career that interests you. Follow this structure:
Paragraph 1 — Introduction: What career are you presenting? Why are you interested in it?
Paragraph 2 — What the job involves: What does a person in this career do every day? What skills and qualifications are needed?
Paragraph 3 — Why it matters: How does this career help the community? Use the passive voice (e.g., “Patients are treated by doctors every day.”).
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 7 response uses cohesive paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence, source awareness and a purposeful conclusion about biography or career presentation.

quizzes.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'ambition' mean?
quizzes.options: A strong desire to achieve something important in life. | An important event or stage in the development of something. | Continuing to work hard even when things are difficult or take a long time. | Something that has been accomplished through effort and skill.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A strong desire to achieve something important in life.
quizzes.explanation: Her ambition is to become a doctor who helps children in rural areas.

quizzes.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'dedication' mean?
quizzes.options: An important event or stage in the development of something. | Being fully committed to a task or purpose; giving time and energy to something. | Excellence that makes someone stand out; a high mark or honour. | Something that has been accomplished through effort and skill.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Being fully committed to a task or purpose; giving time and energy to something.
quizzes.explanation: His dedication to studying every evening helped him pass all his exams.

quizzes.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'perseverance' mean?
quizzes.options: An important event or stage in the development of something. | Continuing to work hard even when things are difficult or take a long time. | Excellence that makes someone stand out; a high mark or honour. | The ability to recover from difficulties and keep going.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Continuing to work hard even when things are difficult or take a long time.
quizzes.explanation: Through perseverance, she learned to speak English fluently.

quizzes.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'achievement' mean?
quizzes.options: A job that requires special training or education. | Excellence that makes someone stand out; a high mark or honour. | Something that has been accomplished through effort and skill. | The ability to recover from difficulties and keep going.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Something that has been accomplished through effort and skill.
quizzes.explanation: Winning the science prize was her greatest achievement this year.

quizzes.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Storm of Truth, part 1': Her family was proud of her _____, and her mother had told her, "We are grateful for every opportunity you earn.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Her family was proud of her ambition, and her mother had told her, "We are grateful for every opportunity you earn.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Success, Careers, and Personal Growth: source text 2': What matters most is not the title you hold but the _____ you bring to your work and the contribution you make to the people around you.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: What matters most is not the title you hold but the dedication you bring to your work and the contribution you make to the people around you.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Success, Careers, and Personal Growth: source text 2': She studied by lamplight every evening, and her _____ impressed everyone who knew her.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: She studied by lamplight every evening, and her perseverance impressed everyone who knew her.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Storm of Truth, part 3': "But a true friend reminded me that integrity is the foundation of every real _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: "But a true friend reminded me that integrity is the foundation of every real achievement.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Storm of Truth, part 1': The winner would receive a scholarship to attend a special leadership academy in Nairobi — a _____ that could change her life forever.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: The winner would receive a scholarship to attend a special leadership academy in Nairobi — a milestone that could change her life forever.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Success, Careers, and Personal Growth: source text 2': When she passed her exams with _____, the entire neighbourhood celebrated.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: When she passed her exams with distinction, the entire neighbourhood celebrated.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model disciplinary language aloud, teach annotation and source checking explicitly, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on evidence, cohesion, viewpoint and vocabulary precision while increasing learner independence.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A strong desire to achieve something important in life. Her ambition is to become a doctor who helps children in rural areas.
Being fully committed to a task or purpose; giving time and energy to something. His dedication to studying every evening helped him pass all his exams.
Continuing to work hard even when things are difficult or take a long time. Through perseverance, she learned to speak English fluently.
Something that has been accomplished through effort and skill. Winning the science prize was her greatest achievement this year.
An important event or stage in the development of something. Finishing primary school is a major milestone in a student’s life.
Excellence that makes someone stand out; a high mark or honour. She passed her exams with distinction and was praised by all her teachers.
The ability to recover from difficulties and keep going. The resilience of the community helped them rebuild after the flood.
A job that requires special training or education. Teaching is a respected profession that requires years of training.
A strong feeling of being suited to a particular career or calling. She felt a vocation for nursing from a very young age.
A person learning a trade or skill from an experienced worker. The young apprentice learned to build furniture from the master carpenter.
